info.instructions:
• Task: Read the source text and competing translations. Highlight all errors. Rate each translation (each column is an output from a single model/translator). At the end, click Next in top-right corner.

  • Highlighting errors:
    • Select text containing an error to mark it as Minor.
    • Click selected text to change the error to Major or remove the highlight.
    • Missing text: Highlight the [MISSING] tag if the translation omits important source text.

  • Error severity:
    • Minor: Imperfections or stylistic issues that do not impact the core message (e.g., awkward phrasing).
    • Major: Confuses meaning, misrepresents the source, or violates the message (e.g., incorrect information, confusing wording).

  • Important rules:
    • Multiple errors: Use separate highlights for each error.
    • Hallucinations: Highlight unsupported extra text; mark as Major.
    • Wrong language: Highlight the entire text, mark as Major, and assign a score of 0.
    • Consistency: Check translation consistency (e.g., technical terms) across the document.

  • Rating scale (0-100%):
    • 85-100% (Very Good): Complete meaning transfer; perfectly natural; no or minimal proofreading.
    • 65-80% (Good): Near-complete transfer, minor inaccuracies; mostly natural, minor awkwardness; needs light proofreading.
    • 45-60% (Acceptable): Main ideas conveyed, noticeable inaccuracies or omissions; uneven naturalness, awkward phrasing; usable only after substantial revision.
    • 25-40% (Borderline): Partial transfer; frequent misinterpretation or omission confusing the message; often unnatural; requires major rewrite.
    • 0-20% (Not acceptable): Violation of meaning; large portions mistranslated, missing, or incoherent; unusable without complete retranslation.

  • For unexpected problems and remarks, use comment box in ⚙️ (top right).

payload.src: 英マンチェスター大学のケイティ・ジョイ博士は、嫦娥5号が持ち帰る新たなサンプルは、月の火山の歴史についても理解を深めることにつながるだろうと話した。

tgt.model-tutorial-A: Dr. Katie Joy of the University of Manchester in the UK said that the new samples brought back by Chang'e 5 will also lead to a better understanding of the history of lunar volcanoes.
tgt.model-tutorial-B: Dr. Katie Joy of the University of Manchester in the UK said that the old samples brought back by Chang'e 5 will also lead to a better understanding of the future of lunar volcanoes.

payload.instructions:
Annotating more than one translation for a source text:

Here you are given two translations of the same source. You need to follow the rules from the previous examples and mark the error spans, assign severities, and give a 0–100% score to each translation separately. Remember lower quality translations should be assigned lower scores.
One of the translations is accurate. Set the score to 90-100%.

The other translation has two major errors. The words "新た" (new) and "歴史" (history) are translated incorrectly to "old" and "future", respectively. Having two major errors, set the score to 10-25%.

payload.src: 「今回のミッションは、過去に火山の噴火があったとわかっている地域に向かっている。正確にいつ噴火が起きたのか、知りたいと思っている」と、ジョイ博士はBBCニュースに語った。

tgt.model-tutorial-A: "This mission is heading to an area where we know there have been volcanic eruptions and water sources in the past. We want to find out exactly when those eruptions occurred," Dr. Joy told BBC News.
tgt.model-tutorial-B: "This mission is heading to an area where we know there have been volcanic eruptions in the past. We want to find out exactly where those eruptions occurred," Dr. Joy told BBC News.

payload.instructions:
Document-level annotation

In some cases you are given multiple source texts from the same document. While annotating each target text, keep in mind the dependencies across source texts when identifying error spans.

Now let's walk through the following example. In one translation, "and water sources" is a hallucinated phrase that does not appear in the source text. Mark the entire phrase as a major error span and set the score to 0. In the second translation, "いつ" is mistakenly translated as "where". Since this changes the meaning of the sentence, mark it as a major error and set the score to 50–60%.
